segments.paradiso-3-g58: Li is the plural masculine definite article (older form of 'i'/'gli' used here before a word starting with a consonant); 'nostri' is the possessive adjective 'our' matching the plural masculine noun 'affetti'; 'affetti' is a plural noun meaning emotional feelings or affections and is the subject of the relative clause that follows.
segments.paradiso-3-g59: This is a relative clause introduced by 'che' ('who/which') referring back to 'affetti'. 'solo' (only) restricts the following predicate; 'infiammati' is a past participle used adjectivally ('inflamed/kindled') and agrees with the plural subject; 'son' is an archaic short form of 'sono' (present indicative, 'are'). 'nel' = 'in the' and 'piacer de lo Spirito Santo' means 'the pleasure/delight of the Holy Spirit' (old orthography 'de lo' = modern 'del').
segments.paradiso-3-g60: 'Letizian' is a present-tense verb (3rd person plural) meaning 'they rejoice' or 'they are glad'—an older poetic form; 'del' = 'of the'; 'suo' is a possessive ('his/its') referring back to the Spirit; 'ordine' is 'order' or 'rank/hierarchy'; 'formati' is a plural past participle agreeing with 'affetti' and means 'formed/shaped' — together: 'they rejoice, formed by his order.'
segments.paradiso-3-g61: 'E' = 'and'; 'questa sorte' = 'this lot/fate/condition' (feminine noun 'sorte'); 'che' is a relative pronoun 'which'; 'par' is the poetic present 'seems' (from 'parere'); 'giù' literally 'down' used adverbially to mean 'low' or 'here below'; 'cotanto' intensifies 'to such an extent'—together: 'and this lot, which seems so lowly.'
segments.paradiso-3-g62: 'Però' here = 'for that reason'/'therefore'. 'n’' is the clitic 'ne' (here meaning 'of it' or 'about it') referring back to the fate/lote; 'è data' is a present passive construction ('is given' / 'has been assigned') with 'data' as past participle agreeing in gender with 'sorte' conceptually — literally 'for that reason, of it is given'.
segments.paradiso-3-g63: 'Perché' = 'because'. 'Fuor' here is an archaic/dialectal form that functions as a past auxiliary (historically from 'furon'/'furono'), so the phrase 'fuor negletti' should be read as 'were neglected' — 'negletti' is the past participle 'neglected'. The subject 'li nostri voti' ('our vows') follows and is plural masculine; word order is inverted for poetic rhythm.
segments.paradiso-3-g64: The coordinating 'e' = 'and'. 'Vòti' (accented in the manuscript) is again 'vows'/'prayers' (same noun as before). 'In alcun' = 'in some/a certain'; 'canto' here most likely means 'corner/quarter/place' (an older meaning of 'canto'), so the phrase indicates the vows were voided or left in some quarter/place. The line is elliptical—the verb understood from the previous clause.
segments.paradiso-3-g65: Ond' is an archaic/poetic form meaning 'thereupon/so' or 'thus'; it introduces the speaker's reply. The sequence io a lei places the subject (io) before the dative (a lei = 'to her'), a common word order in Dante when introducing direct speech.
segments.paradiso-3-g66: Ne' = 'in' (poetic/nearly = ne + elided article or adverbial 'ne' = 'in/among'). The phrase mirabili aspetti vostri = 'your marvelous looks' shows Dante's flexible word order (adjective and possessive surrounding the noun). Risplende is a present intransitive 'shines', and the apparent subject comes after it: non so che divino ('some divine something I do not know') where non so che is an indefinite formula ('I do not know what / some indefinable'), followed by a relative che = 'which' introducing che vi trasmuta da' primi concetti ('which transforms you away from your first conceptions'). Vi is the clitic object 'you' (or 'in you') and trasmuta is 3rd-person singular present 'transmutes/changes'. Da' = da + elision = 'from'.
segments.paradiso-3-g67: Però here functions as a connective ('therefore/so'), linking the preceding observation to the speaker's reaction. Non fui a rimembrar uses passato remoto fui ('I was') with a + infinitive (a rimembrar) to express being disposed or ready to remember; in context it means 'I was not quick to call it to mind.' Festino is used by Dante in the sense 'with haste'/'in haste' (an archaic use derived from festinare); the dictionary senses given in the word-list do not match this verbal/adverbial sense.
segments.paradiso-3-g68: Ma or = 'but now' (ma = but, or = now). M' aiuta is the present aiuta ('helps') with the enclitic m' = mi ('to me'), so 'now what you tell me helps me'; ciò che introduces a content clause ('that which/what'), and tu mi dici is straightforward present 'you tell me' with mi as the indirect object 'to me'. Note the clitic m' attaches to the verb as normal in Italian.
segments.paradiso-3-g69: Sì che = 'so that' introducing a result clause. Raffigurar is an infinitive used as the conceptual subject ('to portray'/'to depict'). The clitic m' here is the dative 'to me' attached to the verb phrase (m'è = 'is to me'), so raffigurar m'è = 'to portray is to me'. Più latino is the predicate 'more Latin' — i.e. 'easier/more natural for me in Latin' — Latin being used figuratively for rhetorical ease or Latinate diction.
segments.paradiso-3-g70: "Ma" is the coordinating conjunction 'but' (here a discursive particle introducing a new question). "Dimmi" is the imperative of dire with the enclitic object pronoun mi ('tell me'), literally 'tell me' addressed to one person (imperative + clitic attached).
segments.paradiso-3-g71: "Voi" is the plural subject pronoun 'you' (emphatic here). "Che" is a relative pronoun 'who' linking to voi. "Siete" is the present indicative, second person plural of essere ('are'). "Qui" is the adverb 'here'. "Felici" is the plural adjective 'happy/fortunate' agreeing with voi; together the phrase means 'you who are happy here.'
segments.paradiso-3-g72: "Disiderate" is the verb meaning 'do you desire/wish' in the second person plural present (Dante's spelling here); it introduces the question. The repeated "voi" is an emphatic subject pronoun. "Più alto loco" is a comparative nominal phrase 'a higher/loftier place' (più modifies alto). Each "per" introduces a purpose clause ('in order to') followed by an infinitive: "per più vedere" = 'to see more' and "per più farvi amici" = 'to make more friends for yourselves' (farvi = fare + enclitic vi, 'to make for you' / 'to gain you'). The parallel structure (per ... e per ...) expresses two purposes.
segments.paradiso-3-g119: Quest’ is the elided form of 'Questo' (this); è is the present copula 'is'. 'la luce de la gran Costanza' = 'the light of great Costanza', where 'de' is the poetic/archaic form of modern 'di' (of) and 'gran' is the aphetic (shortened) form of 'grande'. The relative pronoun che introduces a defining relative clause modifying 'Costanza'; 'del secondo vento di Soave' = 'of/from the second wind of Soave' (del = di + il). 'generò' is the passato remoto, 3rd person singular of 'generare' meaning here 'begot/produced' (a completed past action). The coordinated phrase '’l terzo e l’ultima possanza' gives two objects ('the third and the last power'); the elided articles ('’l', 'l’') are the definite article 'il' before vowel or in poetic elision, and 'possanza' is an archaic form equivalent to modern 'potenza' (power, might).
segments.paradiso-3-g120: 'Così' is an adverb meaning 'thus' and comes before the verb for emphasis. 'Parlommi' is a poetic, enclitic form: passato remoto (3rd sg.) of parlare plus the clitic 'mi' (to me), so literally 'he spoke to me.'
segments.paradiso-3-g121: 'e' is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; 'poi' is a temporal adverb 'then/afterwards' introducing the next action; 'cominciò' is passato remoto 3rd sing. of cominciare 'began'; 'Ave' is the quoted vocative interjection 'Hail!' that begins the prayer.
segments.paradiso-3-g122: The quoted name 'Maria' completes the formula 'Ave, Maria' and the following 'cantando' is a gerund/participle ('singing') showing simultaneous manner — he began the words while singing. The closing quote and comma mark the end of the phrase.
segments.paradiso-3-g123: 'e' links this clause; the repeated 'cantando' is again a gerund indicating manner ('singing'); 'vanio' is an adjectival/poetic use meaning 'vain/ineffectual' (here adverbial in force: singing in a faint/ineffectual way). 'come' introduces a simile; 'per' expresses motion through a medium ('through'); 'acqua cupa' = 'dark/deep water' (adjective after noun for poetic tone); 'cosa grave' = 'a heavy/weighty thing' — the whole simile compares the sound to a heavy object moving through dark water. Word order and choice are poetic and somewhat inverted for rhythm.
segments.paradiso-3-g124: 'La' is the definite article 'the'; 'vista' is the noun 'sight' (here personified as the subject); 'mia' is the possessive 'my'. In poetry the possessive follows the noun ('vista mia') rather than the more prosaic 'mia vista'.
segments.paradiso-3-g125: This relative clause ('che ... seguio quanto possibil fu') modifies 'la vista mia': 'che' = 'which'; 'tanto ... quanto' is a correlative structure meaning 'as much ... as' or 'so much ... as' — here 'which followed her so much as was possible.' 'Seguio' is passato remoto (3rd sg.) of seguire 'followed'; 'possibil' is a poetic contraction of 'possibile'; 'fu' is passato remoto of essere 'was' and completes the phrase 'as much as possible.'
segments.paradiso-3-g126: 'Poi che' here functions temporally as 'after'/'afterwards that'; 'la' is the direct object pronoun 'her' (referring back to Beatrice); 'perse' is passato remoto of perdere 'lost' (the sight 'lost' her — i.e., ceased to behold her).
segments.paradiso-3-g127: 'Volsesi' is an archaic/reflexive past form (poetic contraction of volgersi: volse + si) meaning 'turned itself' (the sight turned). 'al' = 'to the'; 'segno' = 'sign' or token; 'di maggior disio' = 'of greater desire' — 'maggior' (greater) modifies 'disio' (desire), so the sight turns toward the sign that evokes a stronger longing.
segments.paradiso-3-g128: 'e' links the clause; 'a Beatrice' = 'to Beatrice'; 'tutta' ('entirely/wholly') intensifies the reflexive verb 'si converse' (an archaic form of 'si volse/converte') meaning 'turned itself toward.' The reflexive 'si' marks that the sight is turning itself, and the verb form is poetic/past.
segments.paradiso-3-g129: 'Ma' introduces a contrast 'but'; 'quella' ('that one') refers back to the sign/thing just mentioned; 'folgorò' is passato remoto of folgorare meaning 'flashed' or 'dazzled' (struck with a bright flash); 'nel mio sguardo' = 'in my gaze'. 'Sì che' = 'so that' (introducing a result clause); 'da prima' = 'at first/initially'; 'il viso non sofferse' = 'the face did not endure/withstand it' — 'sofferse' is passato remoto of soffrire used in the sense 'to endure/withstand.'
segments.paradiso-3-g130: 'E ciò' = 'and this/that'; 'mi' is the indirect object pronoun 'to me'; 'fece' is passato remoto of fare 'made/caused'; the older construction 'fece a dimandar' uses 'a' + infinitive (here 'dimandar' = 'to ask') — so 'this made me to ask'; 'più tardo' is an adverbial phrase 'later'/'with delay' (literally 'more late'), meaning 'it caused me to delay my question.'
segments.paradiso-3-g104: Dal = da + il; here the contracted preposition means "from the." mondo is the noun "world" referring to worldly/earthly life. Placed first for emphasis, together they set the origin/place from which the action that follows takes place.
segments.paradiso-3-g105: per seguirla is an infinitival phrase meaning "in order to follow her"; per + infinitive indicates purpose. The clitic -la on seguirla is the direct object "her," attached to the infinitive as is normal in Italian.
segments.paradiso-3-g106: giovinetta is a nominative/absolute noun "young girl/maiden." fuggi’ is the archaic past form (fuggì) "fled," and the enclitic mi is the direct object "me" (she fled from me / fled me). The unusual word order (noun before verb+clitic) is poetic but straightforward: "the young girl fled me."
segments.paradiso-3-g107: This coordinate clause combines two past actions by the speaker: nel (in + il) "in the/into the" introduces luogo/instrumental context; suo abito = "her habit/garment" (here specifically a religious habit); mi chiusi is a reflexive past (mi chiusi = "I shut/enclosed myself"). promisi is the first-person past "I promised," and la via de la sua setta = "the way of her sect/order." Together: "and I shut myself into her habit and promised the way of her sect."
segments.paradiso-3-g108: Uomini poi places the subject 'men' with the adverb poi ('then'/'afterwards')—a temporal marker showing what happened next. The comma marks a pause before the qualifying phrase that follows.
segments.paradiso-3-g109: a mal più ch’a bene usi is a comparative expression: a X più che a Y = "to X more than to Y." a mal = "to evil/ill" and a bene = "to good," while usi (pl. of uso) means "practices/customs" or "habits." The whole phrase means "more given to ill than to good customs."
segments.paradiso-3-g110: fuor mi rapiron de la dolce chiostra: fuor (poetic form of fuori) = "out," mi = "me," and rapiron is the archaic past-tense form (rapirono) "they seized/snatched." de la = "from the," dolce chiostra = "sweet cloister" (cloister/convent). Literally: "they seized me out from the sweet cloister."
segments.paradiso-3-g111: Iddio si sa is an impersonal/reflexive construction with Iddio as the subject: "God knows" (si sa = "one knows/it is known" but with Iddio gives personal sense). qual poi mia vita fusi = "what then my life fusi"; qual = "what," poi = "afterwards," mia vita = "my life." The final word fusi is archaic/unusual here (see note): it conveys what befell or how the speaker's life was ruined/ended after those events.
segments.paradiso-3-g112: E quest’ altro splendor: quest’ is an elision of questo/questa ('this'), followed by altro ('other') modifying splendor ('radiance'). che ti si mostra is a relative clause: che = 'that', ti is the indirect-object 'to you', and si mostra is a pronominal (reflexive/mediopassive) present verb meaning 'is shown/appears' (literally 'shows itself to you'). da la mia destra parte places the source/location ('from my right-hand side') with the preposition da + definite article. e che s’accende uses the enclitic s’ (si) with accende (present tense, 'lights/kindles') to mean 'which lights up/is kindled', and di tutto il lume de la spera nostra uses di (here 'by/with/of') + tutto il lume ('all the light') and the archaic de + spera (spera = sfera, 'sphere') + nostra ('our') to indicate the source of the illumination.
segments.paradiso-3-g113: ciò ch’io dico di me: ciò is the demonstrative 'that/this (thing)', ch’ is the elided che 'that/which', io dico is present tense 'I say', and di me ('about me') marks the object/topic of what is said. The whole clause = 'that which I say about myself.'
segments.paradiso-3-g114: di sé intende: di sé = 'regarding herself' (sé = herself/itself), and intende is 3rd-person present of intendere meaning 'understands' or 'takes (it) to mean'. Grammatically this is a short clause: 'she understands it of herself' = 'she takes it as applying to herself.'
segments.paradiso-3-g115: sorella fu,: predicate-noun + past-tense verb. Sorella ('sister') is the predicative noun and fu is the perfect/simple past of essere ('was'); the order (noun before verb) is a stylistic inversion typical of poetry.
segments.paradiso-3-g116: e così le fu tolta di capo l’ombra de le sacre bende: le is the dative clitic 'to her', fu tolta is a periphrastic passive ('was taken away/removed'), and di capo means 'from the head' (literally 'of head'). l’ombra de le sacre bende = 'the shadow/veil of the sacred bands/veils' with the archaic de = di. Overall: 'and so the shadow of the sacred bands was taken from her head.'
segments.paradiso-3-g117: Ma poi che pur al mondo fu rivolta: Ma 'but'; poi che pur = 'after that, even/indeed' (pur adds emphasis); al mondo = 'to the world' or 'back into earthly life'; fu rivolta is a passive/preterite ('was turned/returned'). contra suo grado e contra buona usanza = 'against her inclination/will and against good/customary usage' (grado here means will/pleasure). The clause explains the circumstance of her being returned to the world.
segments.paradiso-3-g118: non fu dal vel del cor già mai disciolta.: non fu = 'was not' (negated past), dal vel del cor = 'from the veil of the heart' (dal = da + il), già mai is an emphatic adverbial pair often rendering 'ever/afterward' in a negative context ('never afterward'), and disciolta is the feminine past participle 'loosened/released' agreeing with the (feminine) subject. Together: 'the veil over the heart was never loosened.'
segments.paradiso-3-g73: This segment is just a punctuation mark (a period). It does not contain lexical items to analyse; it ends the previous speech or line.
segments.paradiso-3-g74: "Con quelle altr' ombre" is a prepositional phrase (Con + determiner + adjectival form + noun) meaning 'with those other shades'; the elided apostrophe in altr' marks a poetic contraction of 'altre'. "Pria" (an adverb) modifies the verb and means 'first/beforehand', and "sorrise" is the passato remoto (3rd sing.) of sorridere, used in narrative/poetic past: 'she smiled'. "Un poco" is an adverbial phrase 'a little'. The implied subject is 'she' (one of the blessed), understood from context.
segments.paradiso-3-g75: "Da indi" is a temporal/adverbial phrase meaning roughly 'thereupon' or 'after that' (literally 'from there'); it links this clause to the previous action. "Mi" is the indirect object pronoun 'to me'. "Rispuose" is the passato remoto (3rd sing.) of rispondere, 'answered', and "tanto lieta" is a predicative adjectival phrase 'so/very happy', describing the manner of the answering.
segments.paradiso-3-g76: The clitic "ch'" (= che) introduces a subordinate clause 'that...'. The construction "arder parea" uses the infinitive arder ('to burn') governed by the imperfect/poetic parere (parea = 'seemed'), so the sense is 'seemed to be burning'. "D'amor" marks cause or quality ('with/of love'), and "nel primo foco" is a locative phrase 'in the first flame/fire' (the first heavenly fire).
segments.paradiso-3-g77: A simple vocative: "Frate" addresses the addressee as 'Brother'. In poetry the vocative stands alone and is set off by the following speech.
segments.paradiso-3-g78: This is a nominal phrase in apposition: "la nostra volontà" ('our will') followed by the adjective "quïeta" ('tranquil/quiet') modifying volontà. Immediately after, "virtù di carità" names or defines that will as the 'virtue of charity' (virtù in apposition). The diaeresis in quïeta marks pronunciation across syllables in the original orthography but does not affect meaning.
segments.paradiso-3-g79: The relative pronoun "che" introduces a clause that further explains 'virtù di carità'. "Fa" is the present tense (3rd sing.) of fare used causatively: 'makes' or 'causes'. "Volerne" is an infinitive with the enclitic pronoun "-ne"; here it means 'to desire it/of it' where the "ne" stands for 'of other things' or 'of what is under discussion'. "Sol" (poetic form of solo) is 'only', and "quel ch'avemo" is a fused relative clause meaning 'that which we have' (ch' = che, avemo = abbiamo, present indicative 'we have'). Therefore the clause means 'which makes us desire only that which we have.'
segments.paradiso-3-g80: Conjunction "e" links this clause to the previous one. "D'altro" (di + altro) means 'of/for anything else'; the negative construction "non ci asseta" uses the indirect object pronoun "ci" ('us') and the verb "assetare" in the 3rd sing. (asseta) meaning 'to make (one) thirsty' or idiomatically 'to crave/want'. Together: 'and nothing else makes us thirsty' = 'we desire nothing else.'
segments.paradiso-3-g81: "Se disïassimo" is a conditional clause formed with the imperfect-subjunctive form disïassimo (old/poetic spelling of 'desiderassimo' or 'disiassimo'), producing a contrary-to-fact or hypothetical sense 'if we were to desire/were to wish'. "Esser" is the infinitive 'to be' and governs the complement "più superne" ('more supernal/heavenly'); the phrase as a whole means 'If we wished to be more heavenly.'
segments.paradiso-3-g82: "Foran discordi" uses an archaic/poetic form 'foran' (literally 'outside' or 'would be outside/at variance') with the predicate adjective "discordi" ('discordant/at odds'). The subject "li nostri disiri" ('our desires') follows in typical Italian inverted order. "Dal voler di colui che qui ne cerne" is a prepositional phrase 'from the will of him who here discerns/judges us'; "cerne" is present indicative 'he discerns' (the verb used in the sense 'discern/judge'), and "ne" refers back to those souls/subjects under consideration.
segments.paradiso-3-g83: The conjunction "che" introduces an explanatory clause: "vedrai" is future (2nd sing.) 'you will see'. It takes the infinitival clause "non capere" ('not to be contained/fit') as complement; here "capere" means 'to be contained' rather than 'understand'. "In questi giri" ('in these circles/courses') locates where 'it' will not be possible to contain/fit something (i.e. different levels/spheres).
segments.paradiso-3-g84: Here "s'" is an archaic contraction introducing the infinitival substantive clause "s'essere in carità" = 'that being in charity' (functioning as the grammatical subject). "È" is the present indicative 'is', "qui" is 'here', and "necesse" (necesse) means 'necessary'. The whole clause states that 'being in charity is necessary here.'
segments.paradiso-3-g85: "E se" introduces a conditional 'and if'; here it means 'and if you ...'. "La sua natura" is 'his nature' (la + possessive + noun). "Ben" is the adverb 'well' modifying the verb "rimiri", which is a present/subjunctive or hortative form of rimirare meaning 'to consider/look at closely'; the clause means 'and if you consider his nature carefully.'
segments.paradiso-3-g86: Anzi = 'rather/on the contrary', an adversative particle. è formale = present indicative of essere + adjective formale used predicatively ('is fitting/proper'). The infinitive phrase tenersi ... follows formale and expresses what is fitting: tenersi is the reflexive infinitive 'to keep oneself / to remain', with the reflexive clitic -si; dentro a la divina voglia = prepositional phrase 'within the divine will' (dentro a = 'inside/within'). 'ad esto beato esse' is an older word order: ad + demonstrative esto + adjective beato + noun/pronoun esse — literally 'for this blessed one/being'.
segments.paradiso-3-g87: per ch’ = 'by which/through which/whereby' (per + che). una = 'one' (the unity/result). fansi is an archaic/poetic form of si fanno / si fa (from farsi/fare) meaning 'are made' / 'become' — here a mediopassive: 'become one'. nostre voglie stesse = 'our wills themselves' with stesse emphasizing identity ('the same'). The clause says that, through that divine will, our wills are made one.
segments.paradiso-3-g88: sì che = resultive conjunction 'so that/therefore' introducing the consequence of the previous clause; here it signals the next explanatory/result clause.
segments.paradiso-3-g89: come = 'as' or 'just as'. noi sem = noi + sem, sem is an archaic contraction of siamo ('we are'). di soglia in soglia is an idiomatic repeated expression 'from threshold to threshold' (di = 'from'), i.e. moving from one stage/entrance to the next. per questo regno = 'through/throughout this kingdom/realm' (per = through). The whole phrase describes our progress through the realm.
segments.paradiso-3-g90: a tutto il regno piace = impersonal/use-of piacere: 'it pleases the whole realm' → 'the whole realm is pleased' or 'the whole realm delights in it' (a + dative). com’ a lo re = 'as [it is] to the king' (com' = com'è / as; lo re = the king). che ’n suo voler ne ’nvoglia = relative clause 'who, in his will, inclines us toward it': ’n is contraction of in, suo voler = 'his will/desire', ne is a clitic referring to 'it' or 'of it', and ’nvoglia (archaic form) corresponds to 'invoglia'/'invo-glia' (to incline/desire) — here third-person form meaning 'inclines/causes desire'.
segments.paradiso-3-g36: Però begins the clause meaning 'therefore' or 'so'. Parla, odi, credi are present-tense imperative (tu) commands strung together: 'speak, hear/listen, believe'; con esse uses the preposition con + a feminine plural pronoun (esse) 'with them'. The three verbs are coordinate imperatives giving instructions to the addressee.
segments.paradiso-3-g37: Ché introduces a causal clause ('for/because'). La verace luce is the noun phrase 'the true light'. Che is a relative pronoun 'that/which' introducing the subordinate clause. Le is a feminine plural clitic 'them' (the souls); appaga is present indicative 'satisfies'; da sé = 'of itself/by itself'; non lascia is present indicative negative 'does not allow/permit'; lor is an alternative clitic form 'them' (object); torcer li piedi is an old infinitive construction meaning 'to turn their feet/stray their steps', with li + piedi = 'the feet'.
segments.paradiso-3-g38: E io is simply 'and I'. A l'ombra uses the preposition a + article to indicate movement or attention toward 'the shade'; che parea più vaga is a relative clause with pare(a)-a (imperfect) 'seemed' and vaga used poetically 'willing/pleasant' — di ragionar attaches the infinitive 'to converse' (di + infinitive is a classical/poetic construction meaning 'for speaking/for conversing').
segments.paradiso-3-g39: Drizza'mi is a contracted past action (drizzai mi → drizzàmi) meaning 'I straightened/raised myself' or 'I sat up' — drizza here is reflexive with mi 'myself'. The form is poetic/elided.
segments.paradiso-3-g40: E cominciai is 'and I began' (past indicative, first person singular). Cominciai is the simple past used narratively.
segments.paradiso-3-g41: Quasi com' uom = 'as if a man'; quasi + simile phrase 'as though'. Cui governs the relative sense 'whose' or 'to whom' — here 'a man whose excessive desire is assuaged'. Troppa voglia = 'too great a desire'; smaga is an archaic verb meaning 'assuages/relieves' (poetic use).
segments.paradiso-3-g42: A direct address begins with the vocative O 'O' (oh), followed by ben creato 'well-created' as an epithet and spirito 'spirit' as the vocative noun. Creato is a past participle used adjectivally.
segments.paradiso-3-g43: Che here is a relative pronoun 'who/that' introducing what follows. A' rai di vita etterna = 'at/to the rays of eternal life' (a' = a + the), la dolcezza senti = 'feels the sweetness' where senti is present indicative 'feels' (experience/sense). The final che begins the subordinate clause 'which, if not tasted...'.
segments.paradiso-3-g44: Non gustata is an adjectival phrase 'not tasted' (past participle used adjectivally) describing the sweetness; non negates the participle.
segments.paradiso-3-g45: Non s'intende mai is an impersonal/passive construction: s' (si) + intende 'is understood' with non ... mai = 'is never understood'. The reflexive/impersonal si makes intende passive/impersonal.
segments.paradiso-3-g46: Grazïoso mi fia uses the subjunctive/future notion 'it will be gracious/kind to me' (fia is an older/fixed form of 'will be' in subordinate clauses). Se mi contenti is 'if you satisfy/answer me' (se = if; contenti is subjunctive/imperative sense 'you satisfy'); del nome tuo e de la vostra sorte = 'about your name and about your lot/condition' (del = di + il, de is poetic variant).
segments.paradiso-3-g47: Ond' is a contracted/poetic form meaning 'whereupon' or 'therefore/then' that links to what immediately follows; ella is the subject pronoun 'she' (here poetic). The comma sets off the apposition that follows.
segments.paradiso-3-g48: Pronta e con occhi ridenti: is an adjectival phrase describing the speaker: pronta 'ready/willing', e 'and', con occhi ridenti 'with smiling/laughing eyes' (ridenti = present participle used adjectivally).
segments.paradiso-3-g49: La nostra carità is the subject 'our charity/love'. Non serra porte is 'does not close doors' (serra = closes; porte here is 'doors' though the supplied sense list is mismatched), a giusta voglia = 'to a just/righteous desire' (a + noun phrase). The sentence is saying charity does not refuse proper desire.
segments.paradiso-3-g50: Se non come = 'except as' or 'only except when' (se non + comparative). Quella che vuol simile a sé tutta sua corte is a restrictive relative clause: quella 'that (desire)' che vuol 'which wishes/wants' simile a sé 'to be like itself' tutta sua corte 'its entire court/retinue' — the meaning is that charity will not accommodate a desire that would make others identical to the desiring self.
segments.paradiso-3-g51: I’ (an archaic contraction of io) + fui is the simple past of essere: “I was.” Nel mondo is the usual prepositional phrase “in the world/earthly life.” Vergine modifies sorella (adjective before noun here for emphasis), so vergine sorella = “a virgin sister” (i.e. a nun). The semicolon separates this self-identification from what follows.
segments.paradiso-3-g52: This is a conditional clause introduced by se (“if”). La mente tua is the subject (“your mind/attention”), ben is an adverb (“well/duly”), and sé is a reflexive pronoun referring back to la mente (itself). Riguarda is present indicative of riguardare meaning “regard/consider” — literally “if your mind well regards itself,” idiomatically “if you reflect carefully.”
segments.paradiso-3-g53: Non ... celerà is future tense: “will not conceal.” The clitic sequence mi ti encodes direct object (mi = me) and indirect object (ti = to you); the order mi ti is determined by clitic rules. L’esser is the nominalized infinitive (the being) serving as the logical subject: “my being more beautiful.” Più bella = “more/very beautiful.”
segments.paradiso-3-g54: Riconoscerai is future second person singular: “you will recognize.” Ch’ is a contracted che = “that.” I’ son is an archaic order (i’ = io) with son ( = sono) “I am”; together: “that I am Piccarda.” Piccarda is the proper name (direct identification).
segments.paradiso-3-g55: Che here is the relative pronoun “who/which/that,” beginning a relative clause that describes Piccarda.
segments.paradiso-3-g56: Posta is the feminine past participle of porre agreeing with Piccarda: “placed.” Qui is adverb “here.” Con questi altri beati is a prepositional phrase “with these other blessed ones,” completing the passive/state description.
segments.paradiso-3-g57: Beata sono shows an inverted word order (adjective before verb) meaning “I am blessed.” In la spera uses spera (an archaic spelling for sfera, “sphere”) with the article; più tarda (“more late/later”) describes that sphere as the most remote/outer one. The clause states her blessed state and its location.
segments.paradiso-3-g1: Relative clause introduced by che modifies Quel sol. Pria is an archaic adverb meaning 'earlier/before'; d’amor (di + amor) expresses cause or manner ('with/by love'). Mi is the clitic object and scaldò is 3rd person singular past (passato remoto) meaning 'warmed' (poetic order: determiner + noun + relative clause + verb + object). '’l' is the elided definite article (il).
segments.paradiso-3-g2: Di bella verità is a prepositional phrase ('to/into beautiful truth'); m’avea scoverto uses avea + past participle scoverto (pluperfect-like in style) meaning 'had uncovered/revealed me.' The clitic m’ is direct object attached to the auxiliary.
segments.paradiso-3-g3: Provando e riprovando are gerunds/participles (present participles) used adverbially to indicate repeated action: 'trying/proving and trying again' that modify the previous verb (the act of revealing/testing the appearance). The two verbs form a linked iterative action.
segments.paradiso-3-g4: Il dolce aspetto is the direct object ('the sweet appearance/visage'). Adjective follows determinative + noun order typical in Italian poetry; it links back to what he was testing ('provando e riprovando').
segments.paradiso-3-g5: Simple coordinator e ('and') plus the subject pronoun io (I) preparing the main clause that follows; the pronoun is topicalized for emphasis as is common in Dante.
segments.paradiso-3-g6: Per + infinitive confessar = 'in order to confess.' Corretto e certo are adjectives qualifying me stesso (reflexive object) and express the state he aims to assert about himself; me stesso is the emphatic reflexive 'myself.'
segments.paradiso-3-g7: Tanto quanto si convenne is a measure clause 'as much as was fitting' with si convenne an impersonal/impersonal-past construction 'it was fitting.' Leva' il capo is an archaic/poetic past form (levà/levò contracted here) meaning 'raised the head'; a + infinitive proferer = 'to utter'; più erto uses erto ('upright/lofty') as adverbial/comparative 'more lofty.'
segments.paradiso-3-g8: Contrast introduced by ma ('but'). Visïone (with diaeresis) is 'vision/apparition'; apparve is passato remoto 'appeared.' Che introduces a subordinate clause: ritenne a sé me tanto stretto = 'kept/held me to itself so close' (ritenne = kept/held; a sé = to itself; me = direct object). Stretto is used figuratively 'close/tight.'
segments.paradiso-3-g9: Per + reflexive infinitive vedersi expresses purpose/result: here 'per vedersi' means 'in order to be seen' or 'so that it could be seen' (the vision causes him to be held close in order to be seen). The infinitive is reflexive, giving a passive/reciprocal sense.
segments.paradiso-3-g10: Che begins a result clause: 'that (so) ...' Di mia confession is a genitive/partitive phrase 'of my confession.' Non mi sovvenne is a past (passato remoto) construction meaning 'did not occur to me / did not come to mind' (mi = indirect clitic, sovvenne = 'came to mind').
segments.paradiso-3-g11: Quali begins a simile 'such as' and per + vetri = 'through glass panes'; trasparenti e tersi are coordinating adjectives 'transparent and polished/clean' (tersi used adjectivally from the verb 'to wipe/clean').
segments.paradiso-3-g12: O ver (o ver = 'or rather') introduces the alternative image 'through clear and tranquil waters.' Per + acque is 'through waters'; nitide e tranquille are adjectives 'clear' and 'calm.'
segments.paradiso-3-g13: Non sì profonde che ... is a 'so ... that' clause: 'not so deep that their bottoms are lost.' Sien is present subjunctive used in a subordinate clause after che of degree ('so ... that ...'), here 'may be/are.' Persi (intended 'lost') is the past participle; the poet is saying the waters are not so deep that their bottoms vanish from view.
segments.paradiso-3-g14: Tornan is present plural 'they return/are reflected.' D’i nostri visi = 'from our faces' (literally 'of our faces'); le postille debili sì are 'the faint little spots/highlights' that are reflected — postille is figurative here (small marks/reflections) and debili sì = 'so weak/faint.'
segments.paradiso-3-g15: Che introduces a comparative/detail: 'so faint that a pearl on a white brow does not come any less strongly to our pupils.' Non vien men forte a le nostre pupille is an idiom meaning the visual impression reaches the eye no less strongly.
segments.paradiso-3-g16: Tali vid’ io = 'such (ones) I saw' (vid' is an apocopated vidi, passato remoto 'I saw'). Più facce a parlar pronte = 'more faces ready to speak' (facce + infinitive a parlar indicates readiness to speak).
segments.paradiso-3-g17: Per ch’io = 'wherefore/so I' (introduces consequence). Dentro a l’error contrario corsi uses corsi (passato remoto of correre/andare figuratively 'I ran/entered') to say 'I fell/moved into the opposite error.' A quel ch’accese amor ... is a clause 'to that which kindled love between the man and the spring' (accese = passato remoto 'kindled').
segments.paradiso-3-g18: Sùbito sì com’ io = 'as soon as I' (Sùbito = immediately); di lor m’accorsi is reflexive/ pronominal (accorgersi di) meaning 'I noticed them.' Accorsi is passato remoto 'I noticed/realised.' The clitic m' (mi) marks the reflexive object.
segments.paradiso-3-g19: Quelle stimando = 'estimating/thinking those (to be)'; stimando is a present participle meaning 'judging/considering.' Specchiati sembianti is a doubled descriptive: specchiati (mirrored/reflected) modifies sembianti (appearances/countenances).
segments.paradiso-3-g20: Per veder = 'to see' (in order to see); di cui fosser uses the imperfect subjunctive-style form fosser (archaic subjunctive of essere) in indirect question/relative 'of whom they might be' (i.e., whose faces they were).
segments.paradiso-3-g21: Li occhi torsi is an elliptical/poetic order meaning 'I turned my eyes' (torsi here = 'turned/twisted'); li occhi is the direct object. The verb is proverbial/poetic.
segments.paradiso-3-g22: E nulla vidi = 'and I saw nothing' (nulla = nothing; vidi = passato remoto 'I saw'). Straightforward negative perfective statement.
segments.paradiso-3-g23: E ritorsili avanti dritti = 'and I turned them back straight forward' (ritorsili is a contracted/poetic form combining ritorsi + li = 'turned them back'); avanti dritti = 'straight forward.' Nel lume de la dolce guida = 'into the light of the sweet guide,' a locative phrase.
segments.paradiso-3-g24: Che is the relative pronoun 'who/which/that' linking to the guide; it begins the clause describing the guide.
segments.paradiso-3-g25: Sorridendo is a present participle ('smiling') that modifies the guide and is adverbial in sense: 'who, smiling,...' It indicates simultaneity with the main verb ardea that follows.
segments.paradiso-3-g26: Ardea is the imperfect tense of ardere ('was burning/was alight'), giving a continuous or vivid description. Ne li occhi santi = 'in the holy/blessed eyes' (ne = in/therein, li occhi santi = the holy eyes). The construction places the burning (figurative light/ardor) in the guide's eyes.
segments.paradiso-3-g27: Non ti maravigliar is a negative imperative 'do not be astonished' with the clitic ti (you). Perch’ io sorrida gives the cause with io + subjunctive sorrida (present subjunctive) used in subordinate causal sense: 'that I smile.'
segments.paradiso-3-g28: Mi disse = 'he said to me' (disse = passato remoto 'said'; mi = indirect object 'to me'). Simple reporting clause.
segments.paradiso-3-g29: Appresso il tuo püeril coto is a causal/attendant phrase: appresso (here = 'because of' / 'on account of') + il tuo (your) + püeril (childish) + coto (an old noun meaning a pricking/sting/impulse—contextual nuance 'rashness' or 'petty sting'). The whole phrase explains why he smiles.
segments.paradiso-3-g30: Poi sopra ’l vero ancor lo piè non fida is literal: poi = 'then/afterward'; sopra il vero = 'upon the truth' (i.e., when considering the truth); ancor = 'still'; lo piè ('the foot', a metaphor) non fida = 'does not yet trust/does not trust it.' Fida here is archaic/poetic in function (trust/faith).
segments.paradiso-3-g31: Ma te rivolve = 'but it turns you' (ma = but; te = you; rivolve = present indicative 'turns/redirects'). The verb is transitive with the clitic object te.
segments.paradiso-3-g32: Come suole is a fixed phrase 'as is customary/as it usually does.' Come = 'as'; suole would be present indicative of suolare (to be wont/accustomed), here indicating habit. (suole is an archaic verbal form used by Dante).
segments.paradiso-3-g33: A vòto is an adverbial phrase meaning 'to emptiness/in vain' (a = to; vòto = empty/vain). It completes rivolve: it 'turns you to emptiness.'
segments.paradiso-3-g34: Vere sustanze son ciò che tu vedi is a declarative clause with the subject vero(s) sustanze 'true substances' and the copula son (they are); ciò che tu vedi is a relative clause 'that which you see.' Word order places the predicate before the relative clause for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-3-g35: Qui rilegate per manco di voto = 'here bound/kept (rilegate) for lack of a vow.' Rilegate is a past participle used adjectivally/passively ('bound' or 'tied up'); per manco di voto = 'for want/lack of a vow.'
segments.paradiso-3-g91: E is the simple conjunction 'and.' ’n is the contracted form of in (here marking location/condition: 'in the'); la sua volontade is the feminine NP 'the her/will' (sua agrees with volontade). È is the present indicative of essere linking subject and predicate; nostra pace is the predicate nominative 'our peace' — literal order 'And in her will is our peace.'
segments.paradiso-3-g92: ell’ (ell(a)) is a contracted pronoun/personification (feminine) referring back to the divine will; è is 'is.' quel mare 'that sea' is the NP; al qual is a preposition + relative pronoun ('to which'); tutto si move uses the reflexive/impersonal si with move (movere) meaning 'all things are moved/tend' toward it. The second hemistich, ciò ch’ella crïa o che natura face, is a nominal clause introduced by ciò che 'that which' (ciò + che) and coordinates two sources: crïa (creates — note the dieresis on ï marks separate vowel sound) or (o) che natura face ('that which Nature makes' — face is an archaic 3rd-sg form of fare).
segments.paradiso-3-g93: Chiaro mi fu allor is an impersonal past expression: chiaro 'clear' + mi fu 'it was to me' (literally 'clear to me it was'), allor 'then.' come introduces a content clause 'how/that'; ogne dove 'everywhere/wherever' is a poetic expression; in cielo è paradiso reads literally 'in heaven is paradise' (predicate inversion typical of poetry).
segments.paradiso-3-g94: etsi is an archaic/conjunctive 'although' (contrast). la grazia del sommo ben = 'the grace of the highest/supreme Good' (sommo modifies ben). d’un modo non vi piove uses the meteorological verb piove 'it rains' metaphorically: 'does not rain there in one (the same) manner' — vi = 'there', d’un modo = 'in one way/degree.'
segments.paradiso-3-g95: Ma sì com’ = 'But just as' (sì com' = così come). elli avvien is an impersonal phrase 'it happens' or 'as it happens' (avvien = avviene). Note the poetic elisions (com’ for come).
segments.paradiso-3-g96: s’un cibo sazia is a conditional protasis: s’ stands for se 'if'; un cibo 'a food'; sazia is 3rd-sing present of saziare 'sates' — 'if one food sates.' e d’un altro rimane ancor la gola continues the idea: 'and for another still remains the throat' (idiomatically 'the appetite for another remains'), rimane is present 'remains,' ancor = still.
segments.paradiso-3-g97: che introduces the explanatory/result clause: 'that that one is desired and of that one one is thankful.' quel si chere is an archaic construction where si is reflexive/impersonal and chere (here adjectival/verb-like in older usage) indicates being sought/cherished; di quel si ringrazia uses si + ringrazia 'one gives thanks for that.' The whole line contrasts desire for one thing and gratitude for another.
segments.paradiso-3-g98: così fec’ io is past action: fec’ is the contracted passato remoto of fare (feci) 'I did,' io 'I.' con atto e con parola 'with gesture and with words' explains how he acted — note the doubled con for emphasis.
segments.paradiso-3-g99: per apprender da lei = 'in order to learn from her' (per + infinitive marks purpose). qual fu la tela = 'what the web/cloth was' (qual = which/what; fu = past 'was'). onde non trasse infino a co la spuola is a relative phrase 'from which she did not draw up to the shuttle' — trasse is passato remoto of trarre 'drew/withdrawn'; infino a co la spuola = 'as far as the shuttle' (spuola = weaving shuttle/bobbin).
segments.paradiso-3-g100: Perfetta vita e alto merto inciela is a compact poetic clause: Perfetta vita 'perfect life' and alto merto 'high/lofty merit' are the subjects; inciela (incielare) is a poetic verb 'exalts to heaven' (transitive, here causing donna più sù). donna più sù = 'a lady/humanly 'more up' (higher in heaven)'; word order is elevated and compact as typical in verse.
segments.paradiso-3-g101: mi disse is simple past narration: mi = 'to me', disse = passato remoto of dire 'said/told.'
segments.paradiso-3-g102: a la cui norma = 'to/according to whose rule' (cui here functions as 'whose' governing norma). nel vostro mondo giù = 'in your world below' (giù = down/below). si veste e vela uses the reflexive/impersonal si + present verbs 'veste' (dresses) and 'vela' (veils) to say people (implicitly those in your world) dress and veil themselves according to that norm.
segments.paradiso-3-g103: perché fino al morir si vegghi e dorma = 'so that until dying one may watch and sleep' (perché here introduces purpose/result rather than a causal 'because'). fino al morir = 'up to death'; si vegghi is a subjunctival/poetic form meaning 'one be awake/watch' (vegghi is an archaic form related to vegliare — note the verb form is not the common modern one); e dorma = 'and sleep.' con quello sposo ch’ogne voto accetta che caritate a suo piacer conforma: 'with that spouse who accepts every vow which charity shapes/conforms to its pleasure' — ch’ introduces the relative clause, and conforma is present 'conforms' (caritate here = 'charity' personified).

sentences.paradiso-3-s9: Our affections, which are kindled only in the pleasure of the Holy Spirit, rejoice, formed by his order.
sentences.paradiso-3-s10: And this lot, which seems so lowly, is granted for that reason: because our vows were neglected, and (were) void in some quarter.
sentences.paradiso-3-s11: So I to her: "In your marvelous looks there shines, I know not what divine thing which transforms you from your first conceptions; therefore I was not quick to call it back to mind; but now what you tell me helps me, so that to portray you is easier for me in Latin."
sentences.paradiso-3-s12: But tell me: you who are happy here, do you desire a higher place in order to see more and in order to make more friends for yourselves?
sentences.paradiso-3-s25: This is the light of great Constancy, which from the second wind of Soave begot the third and the final power.
sentences.paradiso-3-s26: Thus he spoke to me, and then began to sing 'Ave, Maria,' and, singing, (it) sounded weak/ineffectual, like a heavy thing moving through dark water.
sentences.paradiso-3-s27: My sight, which followed her as far as it was possible to follow, after it lost her, turned itself toward the sign of greater desire, and wholly turned itself to Beatrice; but that flashed in my gaze so that at first my face could not bear it; and this made me delay in asking.
sentences.paradiso-3-s21: From the world, to follow her, the young girl fled from me, and I clothed myself in her habit and pledged myself to the way of her sect.
sentences.paradiso-3-s22: Then men, more given to evil than to good customs, dragged me out of the sweet cloister: God knows what then became of my life.
sentences.paradiso-3-s23: And this other splendor that appears to you on my right-hand side and that is kindled by all the light of our sphere — what I say about myself, she takes to refer to herself; she was a sister, and thus the shadow of the sacred bands was taken from her head.
sentences.paradiso-3-s24: But when at last she was turned back to the world against her will and against good custom, the veil over the heart was never afterward loosened.
sentences.paradiso-3-s14: With those other shades she first smiled a little; then she answered me so joyfully that she seemed to burn with love in the first flame: "Brother, our will — the tranquil virtue of charity — which makes us desire only what we have, and thirsts for nothing else."
sentences.paradiso-3-s15: If we wished to be more heavenly, our desires would be at odds with the will of Him who here discerns us; for you will see that being in charity is necessary here, if you well consider His nature.
sentences.paradiso-3-s16: Rather, it is fitting for this blessed one to keep himself within the divine will, by which our wills are made one; so that, as we go from threshold to threshold through this realm, the whole realm is pleased, just as the king is who in his will inclines us to it.
sentences.paradiso-3-s5: So speak with them, hear and believe; for the true light that satisfies them by itself will not let them turn their steps.
sentences.paradiso-3-s6: And I, toward the shade that seemed more willing to converse, straightened myself and began, as one whose too-great desire is assuaged: 'O well-created spirit, who at the rays of eternal life feels the sweetness which, if not tasted, is never understood, it will be gracious to me if you satisfy me about your name and about your lot.'
sentences.paradiso-3-s7: Whereupon she, ready and with laughing eyes, said: 'Our charity does not shut its doors to a righteous desire, except in the case of that desire which would wish its whole court to be like itself.'
sentences.paradiso-3-s8: I was in the world a virgin sister; and if your mind duly considers itself, my being the fairer will not hide me from you, but you will recognize that I am Piccarda, who, placed here with these other blessed ones, am blessed in the outermost sphere.
sentences.paradiso-3-s1: That sun which earlier warmed my breast with love had revealed me to fair truth, testing and testing again the sweet aspect; and I, in order to confess myself correct and certain as much as was fitting, lifted my head to speak more boldly; but a vision appeared that held me so close to itself, in order to be seen, that my confession did not come to mind.
sentences.paradiso-3-s2: Just as through transparent and polished panes of glass, or rather through clear and tranquil waters, not so deep that their bottoms are lost, the faint little highlights of our faces are reflected, so faint that a pearl on a white brow is no less strong to our pupils; such faces I saw more ready to speak; whereupon I, falling into the opposite error, turned to him who kindled love between the man and the spring.
sentences.paradiso-3-s3: As soon as I perceived them, those I judged to be mirrored countenances, to see of whom they were, I turned my eyes; and I saw nothing, and I turned them back straight forward into the light of the sweet guide, who, smiling, kindled in the holy eyes.
sentences.paradiso-3-s4: "Do not be astonished that I smile," he said to me, "because of your childish prick (puerile coto); for upon the truth the foot still does not trust itself, but it turns you, as it is wont, to emptiness: what you see are true substances, here bound for want of a vow."
sentences.paradiso-3-s17: And in her will is our peace: she is that sea to which all things move — what she creates or what Nature makes.
sentences.paradiso-3-s18: Then it became clear to me how, wherever in heaven there is paradise, yet the grace of the supreme good does not fall there in the same way.
sentences.paradiso-3-s19: But just as it happens — if one food satisfies and yet the throat still craves another, one seeks that and thanks the other — so I acted, with gesture and with word, to learn from her what the web was from which she had not drawn up to the shuttle.
sentences.paradiso-3-s20: "Perfect life and lofty merit have exalted her to heaven — a lady higher up," she said to me, "to whose rule in your world below one dresses and veils oneself, so that, until death, one may keep watch and sleep with that spouse who accepts every vow which charity, to its own pleasure, conforms."
